segments.paradiso-1-g58: Two infinitives ('Trasumanar significar') form a nominal phrase meaning 'to pass beyond / to transcend (human limits) — to signify.' 'Per verba' is an ablative/means phrase 'by words' (literally 'through words'). 'Non si poria' is an impersonal negative: 'si' is the impersonal pronoun ('one/it'), and 'poria' is an archaic conditional/possibility verb meaning 'would be possible' or 'could be' (so the clause means 'it could not be [done]'). The whole line therefore reads literally: 'To transcend cannot be signified by words.'
segments.paradiso-1-g59: 'Però' = 'therefore.' 'L’ essemplo' (archaic spelling) = 'the example' (subject). 'Basti' is the third-person singular present subjunctive of bastare used jussively/optatively — 'let ... suffice' or 'may ... suffice.' 'A cui' is a dative relative 'to whom.' The phrase 'esperïenza grazia serba' has inverted word order: 'esperïenza' is the subject, 'grazia' the direct object, and 'serba' the verb 'keeps/preserves' — so, 'to whom experience preserves grace' (i.e. 'for whom experience has kept/preserved grace'). Note the archaic spellings (essemplo, the diaeresis in esperïenza) marking syllable division.
segments.paradiso-1-g60: S’ i’ era sol di me = 'If I were only of myself' (S’ = se/if; i’ is an archaism for 'io' with enclitic article in the manuscript presentation; 'era' is imperfect indicative 'were' used in a conditional/hypothetical sense; 'sol' = 'alone/only'; 'di me' = 'of me'/'by myself'). 'Quel che creasti novellamente' = 'that which you created newly' (relative clause: 'quel' = 'that one/that which'; 'che creasti' = 'that you created' with second-person singular past, 'novellamente' = 'recently/newly'). The whole segment sets up a counterfactual: 'If I were only what you have newly created...'.
segments.paradiso-1-g61: 'Amor che ’l ciel governi' is a nominative apposition describing the force that created him: 'Love that governs heaven.' 'Amor' is the subject 'Love'; 'che' introduces a relative clause; '’l ciel' = 'the heaven(s)'; 'governi' is present subjunctive/relative-habitual form used here as a defining relative meaning 'that governs/rules.' The phrase personifies Love as the divine mover.
segments.paradiso-1-g62: 'tu ’l sai' = 'you know it' (simple present indicative). 'Tu' subject 'you,' '’l' is an object clitic ('it' referring back to the truth just stated), 'sai' present 'know.' This is a direct address to the addressee: 'you know this.'
segments.paradiso-1-g63: 'Che col tuo lume mi levasti' is a subordinate clause introduced by 'che' = 'who/that.' 'Col' = 'con il' = 'with the'; 'tuo lume' = 'your light' (instrumental); 'mi levasti' = second-person singular perfect 'you raised/lifted me' (archaic verb form 'levasti' = 'lifted/removed' in the aorist/perfect sense). Grammatically, it says who (Love) raised the speaker by the addressee's light: 'whom you lifted up with your light.'
segments.paradiso-1-g64: Quando introduces a temporal clause ('when'). la rota is the subject ('the wheel'). che introduces a relative clause; sempiterni is a poetic present (2nd person singular) from sempiternare meaning 'you make everlasting/eternalize', and desiderato is a past participle qualifying rota ('desired' or 'longed-for') placed after the verb by Dante's inverted, poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-1-g65: a sé = 'to itself' (reflexive reference back to la rota). mi fece atteso is a compound expression with fece (past historic of fare, 'made') plus atteso (past participle of attendere used adjectivally) meaning 'caused me to be attentive' or 'made me look toward it'. con l'armonia introduces the instrument ('by/with the harmony'); che temperi e discerni is a relative clause whose verbs are present (2nd person singular) describing what you do to that harmony ('you temper/moderate and you discern/distinguish').
segments.paradiso-1-g66: parvemi is an archaic form equivalent to mi parve ('it seemed to me'); tanto allor = 'so much then'. del cielo acceso = 'of the heaven kindled' where acceso (past participle) modifies cielo. de la fiamma del sol = archaic/decorative di + la: 'by/from the flame of the sun' (de = di). The whole phrase describes the intense, sun‑lit appearance of the heaven.
segments.paradiso-1-g67: che introduces a result/relative clause ('so that'), listing water-phenomena: pioggia o fiume lago (rain or river or lake — Dante uses compact coordination). non fece is the negative past ('did not make'), alcun tanto = 'any so much' (alcun after a negation = 'any'), and disteso (past participle) means 'spread out' or 'extended'. The clause means no rain, river, or lake ever spread out so much.
segments.paradiso-1-g68: La novità del suono e 'l grande lume di lor cagion = two nominative phrases joined by e ('the newness/novelty of the sound and the great light from their cause'). m'accesero is the past historic 3rd person plural of accendere with the clitic mi ('they kindled in me' / 'they set alight in me'). un disio = 'a desire'; mai non sentito uses a double negative idiom meaning 'never felt (before)' (mai + non gives emphatic 'never'); di cotanto acume = 'of so great/so keen an acuteness' (acume = mental sharpness).
segments.paradiso-1-g44: Molto (an indefinite quantity) functions as the grammatical subject, with the copula è + predicative adjective licito meaning 'is permitted/allowed'; the adverbial là ('there') follows at the end. The word order (quantity-subject + verb + predicate + place) is normal in poetic/older Italian and licito is equivalent to modern è lecito.
segments.paradiso-1-g45: Che introduces a relative/result clause referring back to 'Molto' ('that which'); qui 'here' contrasts with là; non negates the verb lece, an archaic present form meaning 'is permitted' (modern è lecito). The prepositional phrase a le nostre virtù marks the indirect beneficiary ('to our powers/faculties') of the permission.
segments.paradiso-1-g46: Mercé here means 'thanks to' or 'by virtue of' and introduces the causal phrase; del loco is 'of/from the place' and fatto is a past participle modifying loco ('the place made'). Per proprio means 'made proper to' (proprio = 'proper to'), and de l’umana spece is a genitival phrase ('of the human species') using the archaic de for modern della.
segments.paradiso-1-g47: Io is the explicit subject; nol is the contracted form of non + lo (not it), a clitic object pronoun attached before the verb. Soffersi is a past tense form meaning 'I endured/suffered (it)' (Dante's older morphology), and molto modifies the extent ('much'/'for long').
segments.paradiso-1-g48: Né introduces a negative continuation 'nor'; sì is an adverb meaning 'so'/'thus' and poco means 'little' — the phrase is elliptical (omitting the verb) and completes the thought: 'nor so little [did I endure it]'.
segments.paradiso-1-g49: Ch’ (che) introduces a result/degree clause: '…nor so little that I would not see it…'. Vedessi is the imperfect subjunctive (congiuntivo imperfetto) used here in a result clause ('I would see' / 'I should see'); nol (non lo) is the object pronoun again; sfavillar is an infinitive complement meaning 'to sparkle' and dintorno is an adverb 'around'.
segments.paradiso-1-g50: Com (come) introduces a simile 'like'; ferro is the noun 'iron'; che links the relative clause; bogliente is an adjective 'boiling/glowing' (used of heated iron); esce is present tense 'comes out' (a timeless/poetic present) and del foco means 'from the fire'.
segments.paradiso-1-g51: E 'and' links the clause; di sùbito (idiomatically 'suddenly') modifies parve, the past tense of parere 'it seemed'; giorno a giorno literally 'day to day' (here 'day upon day') is the phrase perceived as being aggiunto, the past participle 'added' governed by the infinitive essere (parve + infinitive = 'it seemed to be added').
segments.paradiso-1-g52: Come introduces another comparison: quei 'that one' + che 'who' is a relative clause; puote is a present-tense modal 'can/has power' and is followed by the imperfect subjunctive avesse ('might have') in older usage to express potentiality ('could have'). Il ciel d’un altro sole addorno = 'the sky adorned with another sun', where addorno functions as a participial adjective ('adorned').
segments.paradiso-1-g53: 'Beatrice' is the subject. 'tutta' here = 'entirely/wholly' (intensifier modifying fissa). 'ne l’etterne rote' = 'in the eternal wheels' ('ne' = in, 'etterne' = eternal, 'rote' = wheels/celestial spheres). 'fissa' = 'fixed' (adjectival/past participle describing Beatrice). 'con li occhi' = 'with her eyes' (instrumental phrase implying 'with her eyes fixed/intently'). 'stava' = imperfect 'was standing/was' — the verb placing her in that state.
segments.paradiso-1-g54: 'e io' = 'and I' (the poet speaking). 'in lei' = 'on her/about her' (locative: directed toward Beatrice). 'le luci' = 'the lights' (literary for the lights/brightness of her eyes or the lights she manifests). 'fissi' here functions as a verb meaning 'I fixed' (I fixed my gaze on them) — an archaic/poetic verbal form agreeing in sense with the action of the speaker.
segments.paradiso-1-g55: 'di là sù' = 'from there up above' (di = from/of, là = there, sù = up/above). 'rimote' = 'remote/distant' (adjective agreeing with 'le luci' understood from the previous segment), indicating that those lights were far off above.
segments.paradiso-1-g56: Nel suo aspetto is a prepositional phrase ('in her aspect/appearance') placed before the verb for poetic emphasis. Tal modifies the following adverbial dentro ('such / so inwardly'), and mi fei is an archaic past tense (poetic) of fare with the clitic mi as direct object — literally 'I made myself' or 'I became (inwardly) so.'
segments.paradiso-1-g57: Qual introduces a comparison ('as' / 'such as'), and si fé is a reflexive + past of fare (poetic 3rd singular fé) meaning 'he became' or 'he made himself.' Nel gustar uses the infinitive gustar in an adverbial/nominal sense ('in tasting'), and the relative clause che ’l fé consorto... says that the herb (che) 'made him' (’l = lo, 'him') a consort/companion (consorto) 'in the sea of the other gods.' Note the archaic articles/contractions (l', li) and the poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-1-g1: This is the first half of the main clause: La gloria (subject) + di colui che tutto move (a genitive relative phrase: 'of him who moves all'). The verb penetra governs the following prepositional phrase per l’universo; move is present indicative in the relative clause, and colui che introduces a standard Latin-style relative by-phrase 'him who...'.
segments.paradiso-1-g2: This completes the sentence: e risplende is a coordinate verb to penetra ('and it shines'), followed by the locative phrase in una parte 'in one part/place' and the elliptical comparative phrase più e meno altrove meaning 'more in one place and less elsewhere'. The order keeps the subject (la gloria) understood from the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-1-g3: Nel ciel introduces the location ('in the heaven'), followed by a restrictive relative clause che più de la sua luce prende ('which receives more of His light'). The verb fu' io is a poetic inversion of fui io (past simple 'I was'), placing the verb before the pronoun.
segments.paradiso-1-g4: And I saw (vidi) cose (things) followed by a relative clause with a complex negative: che ridire né sa né può chi di là sù discende. Ridire is an infinitive 'to recount', and the clause means 'which neither he who descends from there above knows how to recount nor can'. The double né...né is the classical 'neither...nor' construction.
segments.paradiso-1-g5: Perché introduces a causal clause 'because'; appressando is a present gerund 'approaching/coming near', with sé (itself) and al suo disire ('to His desire'). The gerund gives the reason for the preceding statement.
segments.paradiso-1-g6: Nostro intelletto si profonda tanto: si profonda is a reflexive present construction meaning 'our intellect sinks/becomes deep so much'. Profonda is the verb form (from profondare) used reflexively and tanto indicates degree.
segments.paradiso-1-g7: Che introduces the result clause 'that' (so that): dietro la memoria non può ire literally 'memory cannot go behind/after'—meaning memory cannot follow/keep up. Ire is the archaic infinitive 'to go' (modern andare), and dietro here means 'after' or 'behind'.
segments.paradiso-1-g8: Veramente 'truly' begins a statement; quant' io (short for quanto io) means 'as much as I', and del regno santo ne la mia mente potei far tesoro is an idiom: potei (past 'I could') + far tesoro ('to make a treasure of' = to store/treasure in memory). Ne la mia mente = 'in my mind'.
segments.paradiso-1-g9: Sarà ora materia del mio canto: sarà is future 'will be', ora 'now', materia here means 'subject/topic', so 'will now be the subject of my song'. The construction assigns the material he retained in memory as the poem's subject.
segments.paradiso-1-g10: An apostrophic invocation: 'O buono Appollo' addresses Apollo directly. Buono is an epithet 'good' modifying the god's name; the comma marks the start of the poet's plea.
segments.paradiso-1-g11: a l’ultimo lavoro 'for the final task/work' sets the purpose. Fammi is an imperative (fa' + mi) 'make me'; del tuo valor 'of your power/virtue'; sì fatto vaso 'such a made vessel'—i.e. 'make me such a vessel of your power.' Note some provided lemma/sense entries are nonstandard (fammi is imperative 'make me').
segments.paradiso-1-g12: come dimandi a dar l’amato alloro: come 'as' or 'just as', dimandi (addressing Apollo) 'you demand/require', a dar 'to give', l'amato alloro 'the beloved laurel' (the poetic wreath). The sense is 'as you demand when you give the beloved laurel' — a model for the poet's requested investiture.
segments.paradiso-1-g91: Qui = 'here' indicating place. veggion is an archaic/poetic form of the verb 'vedono' (they see); it is the main verb and governs the direct object that follows. The noun phrase l’alte creature = 'the lofty creatures' (article + adjective + noun). l’orma de l’etterno valore = 'the trace/footprint of the eternal value' (l’orma is the direct object; de + noun expresses the genitive). Word order is typical Italian: subject (creature) after verb, then object phrase introduced by article.
segments.paradiso-1-g92: Il qual è fine = 'which is the end' where 'il qual' is a relative pronoun referring back to 'valore' and 'è' is present indicative of essere. al quale è fatta = 'to which is made' uses the prepositional relative phrase 'al quale' (to which) followed by a passive or resultative periphrasis 'è fatta' (is made/has been made). la toccata norma = 'the touched/appointed rule' is the noun phrase serving as the thing made; 'toccata' is a past participle used adjectivally.
segments.paradiso-1-g93: Ne l’ordine ch’io dico = 'In the order that I speak of'; 'Ne' here means 'in' or 'within' (used with abstract domains), 'ch’io dico' is a relative clause 'that I say'. sono accline = present indicative 'are inclined' (predicate adjective 'accline' agrees with 'tutte nature'). tutte nature = 'all natures' (subject). Note the inversion: the locative phrase opens the sentence.
segments.paradiso-1-g94: per diverse sorti = 'by/through different destinies' where 'per' introduces the cause or means and 'diverse sorti' is the plural noun phrase 'different fates/lot'. The short prepositional phrase modifies how the natures are 'accline'.
segments.paradiso-1-g95: più al principio loro e men vicine = 'more to their beginning and less near'; 'più' and 'men' are comparative adverbs modifying 'vicine' and 'al principio loro' specifies the reference point 'to their principle/origin'. 'Loro' is a possessive referring back to 'nature'.
segments.paradiso-1-g96: onde si muovono a diversi porti = 'whence/therefore they move to different harbors'; 'si muovono' is present reflexive/passive-form verb 'they move' and 'a diversi porti' is the destination. per lo gran mar de l’essere = 'across the great sea of being' where 'per' indicates motion through/over, 'lo gran mar' is the poetic word order for 'the great sea', and 'de l’essere' is the genitive 'of being'.
segments.paradiso-1-g97: e ciascuna con istinto a lei dato che la porti = 'and each one with an instinct given to it that carries it'; 'ciascuna' is the emphatic subject 'each (one)', 'con istinto a lei dato' is an ablative/agent phrase with the past participle 'dato' ('given to her/it'), and the relative clause 'che la porti' uses subjunctive-like sense of purpose/result: 'that carries it' (porti here is present subjunctive/indicative form depending on reading, meaning 'that leads/carrries it toward its goal').
segments.paradiso-1-g98: Questi ne porta il foco inver’ la luna = 'This one carries of it the fire toward the moon.' 'Questi' (masculine demonstrative) identifies one class; 'ne' is a partitive/locative pronoun 'of it/from it'; 'porta' is present indicative 'carries' and 'il foco' is the direct object. 'inver’' (contracted 'inverso') is an archaic preposition meaning 'toward/against', followed by 'la luna'.
segments.paradiso-1-g99: questi ne’ cor mortali è permotore = 'this one is the mover in mortal hearts.' 'questi' again names a class; 'ne’ cor mortali' = 'in mortal hearts' (ne’ = in, elided form); 'è permotore' uses the copula 'è' + noun 'permotore' meaning 'instigator' or 'driving force.' Note the predicate nominative construction.
segments.paradiso-1-g100: questi la terra in sé stringe e aduna = 'this one draws the earth into itself and gathers it.' 'stringe' (present indicative) means 'presses/closes in' and 'aduna' means 'gathers together'; 'in sé' is a reflexive/emphatic phrase 'into itself' and 'la terra' is the object.
segments.paradiso-1-g101: né pur le creature che son fore d’intelligenza quest’ arco saetta = 'nor even the creatures that are outside of intelligence can shoot this bow.' 'né pur' = 'nor even', 'le creature' is the subject, 'che son fore d’intelligenza' is a restrictive relative clause 'that are outside of intelligence/understanding', and 'quest’ arco saetta' is an inverted clause where 'quest’arco' is the instrument and 'saetta' (verb/noun used as verb) means 'shoots arrows' or 'strikes as an arrow.'
segments.paradiso-1-g102: ma quelle c’hanno intelletto e amore = 'but those that have intellect and love.' 'ma' = 'but'; 'quelle' = 'those (ones)'; 'c’hanno' is a contraction where c' stands for 'che' (that) + 'hanno' (they have), so the clause means 'that have'; 'intelletto e amore' are the two complements (intellect and love) that qualify which creatures can 'saetta' the bow.
segments.paradiso-1-g103: La provedenza = 'Providence' with the definite article; subject of the sentence. The noun is personified and will take a relative clause next. This is a simple nominal phrase.
segments.paradiso-1-g104: che cotanto assetta = 'which arranges/sets in order so much.' 'che' is the relative pronoun 'which', 'cotanto' = 'so much/to such an extent', and 'assetta' is a present-tense verb meaning 'sets/arranges' (poetic usage). The clause modifies 'La provedenza.'
segments.paradiso-1-g105: del suo lume fa ’l ciel sempre quïeto = 'of its light it makes the heaven always calm.' 'del suo lume' is a genitive/ablative phrase 'by/with its light'; 'fa' is present indicative 'makes' and '’l ciel' is the direct object 'the sky/heaven'; 'sempre quïeto' is a predicate adjective 'always quiet.' The following 'nel qual...' begins a relative clause describing that heaven.
segments.paradiso-1-g106: e ora lì, = 'and now there,' a brief transitional adverbial: 'e' links to the previous clause, 'ora' = 'now', 'lì' = 'there' (pointing to a determined place/sphere).
segments.paradiso-1-g107: come a sito decreto = 'as to an appointed/ordained place.' 'come' = 'as', 'a' = preposition 'to/at', 'sito decreto' is a poetic expression 'decreed/specified site/place' (sito = place, decreto = decreed/appointed). The phrase explains the manner or destination.
segments.paradiso-1-g108: cen porta la virtù di quella corda che ciò che scocca drizza in segno lieto = 'the virtue/power of that cord brings that thing to us; what it shoots it straightens/aims into a joyful mark.' 'Cen' is a contracted/clitic form meaning 'to us from that/of it' (partitive/adverbial), 'porta' = 'brings', 'la virtù' = 'the power', 'di quella corda' = 'of that cord'. The relative clause 'che ciò che scocca drizza in segno lieto' explains the cord's effect: 'that which it shoots (scocca) it directs (drizza) toward a joyful sign/goal.' Word order is compact and poetic, with multiple clitics.
segments.paradiso-1-g13: Infino a qui is a prepositional phrase meaning 'up to this point' (infino + a + qui). l’un giogo di Parnaso literally 'the one yoke of Parnassus' uses the article + uno for emphasis ('one single yoke') and di Parnaso is a genitive phrase. assai mi fu is an idiomatic past construction: assai = 'enough' and mi fu (fu = passato remoto of essere) = 'it was to me' → 'it sufficed me' or 'was enough for me'.
segments.paradiso-1-g14: ma or = 'but now' (ma = but, or = now). con amendue = 'with both' (amendue = both together). m’è uopo is an impersonal/dative construction (mi + è + uopo) meaning 'it is necessary for me' → 'I must'. intrar is the poetic infinitive 'to enter'; ne + l'aringo rimaso = 'into the remaining aringo' (ne = 'into', l' = the, aringo = public assembly/arena, rimaso = 'remaining').
segments.paradiso-1-g15: Entra is the singular imperative (enter!). nel = in + the (nel) and petto mio places the possessive after the noun as is normal in poetry: 'my breast'. The comma marks the command to enter the speaker's chest.
segments.paradiso-1-g16: e spira tue links a the previous imperative with another command: spira is an imperative 'breathe' and tue (literally 'your') is elliptical for 'your breaths/inspirations'. sì come quando = 'just as when' (sì come = 'as/just as', quando = 'when'). Marsïa traesti is an archaic past form meaning 'you drew/pulled out' (traesti = past of trarre). de la vagina de le membra sue = 'from the sheath of his limbs' (vagina here = 'sheath/scabbard', de = 'from/of', le membra sue = 'his limbs').
segments.paradiso-1-g17: O is the vocative interjection ('O, ...'). divina is the adjective 'divine'; virtù here means 'power/ability' rather than only moral virtue (common in Dante). The whole is a direct address: 'O divine power'.
segments.paradiso-1-g18: se introduces a conditional 'if'. mi ti presti is an unusual reflexive/dative phrasing: ti presti = 'you lend yourself' and mi = 'to me' → 'if you lend yourself to me'; presti is present (here used to express willingness). tanto che = 'so that' introducing a result clause. l’ombra del beato regno segnata nel mio capo = 'the shadow of the blessed realm, marked on my head' where segnata is a past participle agreeing with ombra. io manifesti places the subject pronoun io before manifesti (subjunctive/potential 'may I manifest') for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-1-g19: vedra’mi is the future verb vedrà with the enclitic pronoun mi attached ('you/it will see me'); the apostrophe marks the poetic contraction. al piè = 'to the foot/base'; del tuo diletto legno = 'of your beloved tree/wood' (legno used metaphorically for the laurel/tree of poetry). venire is the infinitive complement: vedrai me venire → 'you will see me come'.
segments.paradiso-1-g20: e coronarmi uses the infinitive coronarmi ('to crown myself') with the enclitic mi. de le foglie = 'with/of the leaves' (de = of/with). che introduces a relative clause: la materia e tu mi farai degno = 'the subject (matter) and you will make me worthy' — la materia and tu are coordinated as the agents that will make the speaker worthy (farai = future 'you will make', mi = me, degno = worthy). The relative ties those leaves to what the matter and the divine power will make him worthy of.
segments.paradiso-1-g21: Sì is an adverbial intensifier meaning “so” (not a yes), rade is an adjective meaning “rare/sparse” agreeing with the feminine plural noun volte; volte is the plural of volta “time/occasion.” Put together, the phrase means “so rare/so few times.”
segments.paradiso-1-g22: Padre is a direct address (vocative) — the speaker is addressing someone as “Father.” It stands alone as an interjection-like noun.
segments.paradiso-1-g23: Se here is the archaic/poetic reflexive/impersonal particle (equivalent to modern si) used to form an impersonal construction; ne is a partitive pronoun (“of it / from it”) referring back to the thing just mentioned; coglie is 3rd person singular present of cogliere “seizes, catches” giving the sense “one/sees/is seized by it.” Per + infinitive (per trïunfare) expresses purpose/aim “to/for triumphing.” The o ... o phrase (“o cesare o poeta”) gives examples in apposition: “whether Caesar (emperor) or poet.” The trema in trïunfare marks pronunciation but does not change grammar.
segments.paradiso-1-g24: A nominal phrase: colpa e vergogna = “fault and shame.” De + l’ is the archaic contraction of delle (“of the”), so de l’umane voglie = “of the human desires.” Umane agrees with voglie (feminine plural). The whole phrase functions as a comment on the preceding clause.
segments.paradiso-1-g25: Che is a relative pronoun (“that/which”) introducing the clause. Parturir is an infinitive (poetic form of partorire/parturire) meaning figuratively “to bring forth”; letizia is its object (“joy”). In su is a poetic double preposition meaning “upon,” and la lieta delfica deïtà is the noun phrase “the glad Delphic deity” (Apollo). Dovria is the modal verb (conditional/optative sense “ought to/should”) and the grammatical subject la fronda peneia (“the Peneian frond,” i.e. the laurel/branch of Peneus) follows it for poetic word order. The clause therefore says that the Peneian branch ought to give birth to joy on the Delphic god.
segments.paradiso-1-g26: Quando = “when/whenever.” Alcun is an indefinite pronoun meaning “anyone / someone.” Di sé is a reflexive phrase meaning “for himself / on his own behalf.” Asseta is the 3rd person singular present of an archaic verb (assetare/assettare in Dante) used here in the sense “is set upon / desires / satisfies himself” — contextually “seeks it for himself” (i.e. desires personal glory).
segments.paradiso-1-g109: 'Vero è che' is a short equative clause with verb before the subordinate 'che'. The inversion is poetic: literally 'True is that...', functioning as the common Italian idiom 'It is true that...' introducing a clause.
segments.paradiso-1-g110: 'come' here means 'just as' and introduces a comparison. 'forma' is the grammatical subject. 'non s’accorda' is present indicative 3rd sg; 's’' is the reflexive/clitic 'si' contracted and 'accorda' = 'agrees/conforms to'. 'molte fïate' is an adverbial expression 'many times' (often) placed between verb and complement; 'a l’intenzion de l’arte' is the indirect object 'to the intention of the art/artist'.
segments.paradiso-1-g111: 'perch’' = 'perché' ('because') gives the reason for the previous statement. 'a risponder' is an infinitive introduced by the preposition 'a' (older style: 'to answer/respond'); 'la materia' is subject and 'è sorda' is a metaphorical predicate 'is deaf' meaning 'does not respond'. The phrase literally: 'because matter is deaf to responding.'
segments.paradiso-1-g112: 'così' (= 'thus/so') links the consequence introduced to the preceding clause. 'da questo corso' is a prepositional phrase 'from this course/track' indicating source. 'si diparte' is present indicative 3rd sg with clitic 'si' forming an intransitive/reflexive sense 'departs/separates'. 'talor' (= 'sometimes') modifies the verb and 'la creatura' is the postpositive subject ('the creature' that departs). Note the slightly marked word order (verb before subject) for poetic rhythm.
segments.paradiso-1-g113: The clitic 'c’' is a contraction (here of 'che') meaning 'which/that'; 'ha' is present indicative 3rd sg of 'avere' ('has'); 'podere' is a noun 'power' (ability); 'di piegar' = 'to bend' (infinitive) where 'di' introduces the infinitival complement. The whole means 'which has the power to bend.'
segments.paradiso-1-g114: 'così' = 'thus/so' and 'pinta' is an adjective (from 'pinto/pintare') meaning 'painted/portrayed'; it post-modifies the creature: 'thus painted' or 'so depicted'. Grammatically it's a short adjectival phrase qualifying the preceding noun clause.
segments.paradiso-1-g115: 'in altra parte' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into another part/place' and completes the idea of motion/direction begun by 'piegar' — i.e. 'bends ... to another place.'
segments.paradiso-1-g116: The clause begins with a comparative 'e sì come' ('and just as') and uses the impersonal construction 'veder si può' ('one can see' / 'it can be seen') where the infinitive 'veder' precedes the clitic 'si' and modal verb 'può'. 'cadere' is infinitive complement 'fall'. 'foco di nube' is a noun phrase 'a fire from a cloud' (an image of sparks or flame falling from a cloud). Word order is inverted for emphasis ('veder si può cadere' rather than 'si può vedere cadere').
segments.paradiso-1-g117: 'sì' (= 'so/thus') recalls the previous comparison. 'l’impeto primo' = 'the first impulse' (article + noun + adjective). The second 'l’' before 'atterra' is an object clitic ('lo' = 'it'), so 'l’atterra' = 'casts it down' or 'overthrows it'. 'atterra' is 3rd sg present (from 'atterrare'/'atterrare' used here meaning 'makes fall'); 'torto' is a past-participial/adjectival sense 'twisted/perverted' modifying the object; 'da falso piacere' indicates the cause/agent 'by false pleasure'.
segments.paradiso-1-g118: 'Non ... ammirar' is the negative of a modal/obligative phrase; 'dei' is an archaic second-person singular modal ('devi' = 'you must / you should') so the sense is 'You should not (already) admire' or 'You must no longer marvel.' 'più' means 'any longer' here. 'ammirar' is the infinitive 'to marvel/admire' governed by the modal 'dei.'
segments.paradiso-1-g119: 'se bene stimo' is a parenthetical conditional 'if I judge well' ('se' = 'if', 'bene' = 'well', 'stimo' = 1st sg present of 'stimare' meaning 'I reckon/think'). It qualifies the speaker's evaluation: 'if I judge rightly.'
segments.paradiso-1-g120: 'lo tuo salir' is a nominal phrase where the definite article 'lo' precedes the possessive 'tuo' because 'salir' (an infinitive used as a noun) is an abstract noun: 'your ascent/your rising.' The article + possessive + infinitive-noun is a common poetic construction.
segments.paradiso-1-g121: 'se non come' = 'unless/as only' (i.e. 'except as'). 'd’un rivo' = 'of a stream' (d' = di + article). The following clause 'se d’alto monte scende giuso ad imo' describes that stream: 'which from a high mountain descends down to the bottom.' 'scende' is 3rd sg present ('it descends') with subject understood (the 'rivo'); 'giuso' = 'down'; 'ad imo' = 'to the very depths/bottom.'
segments.paradiso-1-g122: 'Maraviglia sarebbe in te' uses the conditional 'sarebbe' (3rd sg conditional of 'essere') to say 'It would be a wonder in you' or 'it would be surprising concerning you.' 'in te' means 'in you' (i.e. that would be something surprising about you). The comma and following 'se' introduce the hypothetical condition.
segments.paradiso-1-g123: 'privo d’impedimento' is an adjectival phrase: 'privo' = 'deprived/ free from' and takes the preposition 'di' (here contracted 'd’') followed by 'impedimento' = 'hindrance/obstacle.' Together it means 'free from impediment.'
segments.paradiso-1-g124: 'giù ti fossi assiso' expresses the conditioned circumstance introduced by 'se'. 'giù' = 'down/below'; 'ti' is the reflexive/object clitic 'you'; 'fossi' here is imperfect subjunctive (first part of the compound subjunctive) used in the protasis ('if you had been...') and 'assiso' is a past participle/adjectival 'seated'. The construction 'fossi assiso' = 'you had been seated' in a counterfactual sense.
segments.paradiso-1-g125: 'com’ a terra quïete in foco vivo' is a simile introduced by contracted 'com’' = 'come' ('as/like'). 'a terra quïete' = 'at rest on the earth' (the noun 'quiete' = 'rest/quiet' is used elliptically), and 'in foco vivo' = 'in living fire' (a vivid image). The whole phrase compares the hypothetical seated state to someone at rest on the earth amid a living flame.
segments.paradiso-1-g126: 'Quinci' = 'from here / hence'. 'rivolse' is the passato remoto (3rd sg of 'rivolgere') used narratively = 'he turned'. 'inver’' is an archaic form of 'inver'/'in verso' meaning 'toward'. 'lo cielo il viso' = 'his face toward the sky' where 'lo' is the definite article before 'cielo' and 'il viso' is the direct object; word order places the prepositional phrase before the object for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-1-g27: Poca favilla is the noun phrase 'little spark' (poca modifies favilla). Gran fiamma is the noun phrase 'great flame'. Seconda is a verb (3rd person singular present of secondare, used here poetically) meaning 'follows' or 'comes after'; Dante places the verb at the end for metrical inversion, so the clause reads 'a little spark follows a great flame.'
segments.paradiso-1-g28: Forse = 'perhaps'. Di retro a me = 'behind/after me' (di + retro expresses location/sequence). Con miglior voci = 'with better voices' (an ablative/means phrase: by people with better voices). Si pregherà is an impersonal/passive construction (si + pregherà, future 3rd person of pregare) meaning 'people will pray' or 'there will be prayers'. Perché + subjunctive (risponda) introduces the content or purpose of the prayer: 'that Cirra answer.'
segments.paradiso-1-g29: Surge is 3rd-person singular present of surgere 'rises/ascends'. Ai mortali is a dative phrase 'to mortals'. Per diverse foci 'through various mouths/outlets' (foci = foce, plural; here metaphorical). La lucerna del mondo (the subject) is placed at the end by poetic inversion: 'the lamp of the world rises to mortals through various outlets.'
segments.paradiso-1-g30: Ma = 'but'. Da quella che ... giugne is a relative clause introduced by quella 'that one' (the antecedent); giugne is the 3rd-person singular present of giungere 'arrive/come/reach' used here poetically. Quattro cerchi is the phrase 'four circles' and con tre croci 'with three crosses' expresses accompaniment; word order is somewhat inverted for verse. (Note: in Dante's usage giugne may govern a direct object or indicate arrival.)
segments.paradiso-1-g31: Con miglior corso e con migliore stella are two coordinated instrumental phrases 'with a better course and with a better star' (miglior and migliore are apocopated forms of migliore). Esce is 3rd-person singular present of uscire 'emerges/comes forth'. Congiunta is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'joined' agreeing with the thing emerging: 'it issues forth joined (i.e. accompanied), with a better course and a better star.'
segments.paradiso-1-g32: E la mondana cera = 'and the worldly wax' (cera used metaphorically). Più a suo modo tempera e suggella: più modifies the verbs 'tempera' and 'suggella' (3rd-person singular present: 'tempers' and 'seals'), a suo modo = 'in its own way'. The clause says the worldly wax 'more in its own way tempers and seals' (i.e. it moderates and seals things according to its own fashion).
segments.paradiso-1-g33: Fatto avea is the poetic inversion of aveva fatto 'had made' (past perfect sense: the participle before the auxiliary). Di là mane e di qua sera = 'on that side morning and on this side evening' (mane = morning, sera = evening; di là / di qua mark the two sides). Tal foce = 'such an outlet/mouth' is the object created. Word order is rearranged for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.paradiso-1-g34: E quasi tutto era là bianco = 'and was there almost entirely white' (quasi tutto = almost all; era = imperfect 'was'; là bianco = 'there white' with predicate adjective after the adverb). Quello emisperio (that hemisphere) follows in inverted order as the grammatical subject: 'that hemisphere was almost all white.'
segments.paradiso-1-g35: E l’altra parte nera = 'and the other part (was) black.' Simple nominal phrase: l’altra parte = 'the other part/side'; nera is predicate adjective 'black'. The verb 'was' is understood from the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-1-g36: Quando = 'when'. Beatrice is the subject. In sul sinistro fianco = 'on my left side' (in sul = poetic compound 'on the'; sinistro fianco = left flank/side). Vidi is the 1st-person singular past 'I saw'. Vidi takes rivolta (past participle 'turned') and riguardar (infinitive 'to look/ gaze') as verbal modifiers describing Beatrice: 'I saw Beatrice turned on my left side and gazing at the sun.' Nel sole = 'into/at the sun.'
segments.paradiso-1-g37: Aguglia (here 'an eagle') is the subject. Sì = 'so, thus'; non ... unquanco = 'never' (negative with an emphatic indefinite). Li s’affisse is an archaic past form meaning 'fixed itself to him' (s’ = reflexive, li = dative 'to him' referring to the sun); affisse is a past form used poetically. The whole clause means 'an eagle had never fixed itself upon him so (steadily).'
segments.paradiso-1-g38: 'E' = 'and' linking this simile to what precedes. 'sì come' = 'just as' (introducing a comparison). 'secondo raggio' = 'a second ray' (second = the following ray). 'suole' is present indicative used idiomatically with an infinitive to mean 'is wont' or 'usually does'; it governs the two infinitives that follow. 'uscir del primo' = infinitive 'to issue/come out from the first' ('del' = di + il = 'from the'). 'e risalire in suso' = 'and to rise back up' (risalire + in suso, archaic 'upwards').
segments.paradiso-1-g39: 'pur' here strengthens the comparison ('even/just as'). 'come' = 'as/like'. 'pelegrin' is the archaic form of 'pilgrim'. 'che tornar vuole' is a relative clause: 'che' = 'who/that', 'tornar' = infinitive 'to return', and 'vuole' = present 'wants/wishes' (he wishes to return).
segments.paradiso-1-g40: 'così' = 'so/thus' linking the result to the prior simile. 'de' is the archaic form of 'di' = 'of/from'. 'l’atto' = 'the act/gesture' (atto = an action or motion). 'suo' = 'her/his/its' (possessive referring to Beatrice in context). This is a brief prepositional phrase that starts the clause explaining the source of the effect.
segments.paradiso-1-g41: 'per' = 'through/by means of'. 'li occhi' = 'the eyes' (archaic article 'li'). 'infuso' is a past participle meaning 'poured/infused' and here indicates that her act was poured through the medium of the eyes. 'ne' = 'in/into' (archaic) and 'l’imagine mia' = 'my image/my imagination' (i.e. into my mental image). The whole phrase explains how her act affected the speaker: it was poured through the eyes into his imagination.
segments.paradiso-1-g42: 'il mio' = 'mine' (literally 'the my one' — the speaker's corresponding act/feature). 'si fece' = reflexive/periphrastic past meaning 'was made/became' (si + fece, fece = passato remoto of fare); together they mean 'my [thing] was made (so/as a result)'. This states the effected similarity: what he had was made like hers.
segments.paradiso-1-g43: 'e' = 'and' continuing the narrative. 'fissi' functions here as 'fixed' (a past action/state: she fixed her gaze) — an archaic/poetic form used as a verbal/adjectival form. 'li occhi' = 'the eyes'. 'al sole' = 'on/to the sun' ('al' = a + il). 'oltre nostr’ uso' = 'beyond our usual/customary use' — 'nostr’' is contracted 'nostro' and 'uso' = 'custom/practice' (i.e. beyond what we are accustomed to seeing).
segments.paradiso-1-g69: Ond' is a poetic/contractional adverb meaning 'therefore/so' (from onde) placed before the subject; ella is the emphatic subject pronoun 'she'. Together they introduce the agent of the following action.
segments.paradiso-1-g70: che is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause; vedea is the imperfect indicative (archaic spelling) meaning 'saw'; me is the direct object 'me'; sì com' io is a comparative phrase 'just as I (do/did)', with the subject pronoun io added for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-1-g71: a quïetarmi is an infinitive with the enclitic reflexive -mi meaning 'to calm me' or 'to soothe me' (the diaeresis marks syllabification); l'animo commosso is the object 'my agitated spirit', with commosso a past participle used adjectivally.
segments.paradiso-1-g72: pria che = 'before' (pria + che) introduces a temporal subordinate clause; io is the stressed subject pronoun; a dimandar uses the preposition a + infinitive 'dimandar' ('to ask'), here meaning 'before I could ask' or 'before I asked'.
segments.paradiso-1-g73: la bocca aprio is an archaic past-tense clause: aprio (apriò) is the third-person singular past (passato remoto) 'opened' with the object la bocca; e cominciò 'and (she) began' links immediately to the reported speech.
segments.paradiso-1-g74: Tu stesso is emphatic 'you yourself'; ti fai is present indicative reflexive 'you make yourself / you become'; grosso here is figurative 'thick/dull/obscured' (clouding the intellect); col = con + il 'with the/by the' and falso imaginar = 'false imagination' (imaginar as a noun, 'imagining').
segments.paradiso-1-g75: sì che introduces the result 'so that'; non vedi is present indicative 'you do not see'; ciò che introduces a relative clause 'what'; vedresti is the conditional 'you would see'; se l'avessi scosso is a counterfactual conditional with the auxiliary in the past-subjunctive 'avessi' + past participle 'scosso' = 'if you had shaken it off.'
segments.paradiso-1-g76: Tu non se' in terra uses archaic spelling se' for sei (second-person singular present of essere) so the clause states 'you are not on Earth' with the negation non preceding the verb.
segments.paradiso-1-g77: sì come tu credi = 'just/as you believe' (sì = 'so/just so', come = 'as'), a simple comparative clause in the present indicative addressing the pilgrim's mistaken belief.
segments.paradiso-1-g78: ma introduces contrast 'but'; folgore is a nominative noun 'lightning' or 'thunderbolt' used as the metaphorical subject of the next participial phrase.
segments.paradiso-1-g79: fuggendo is a present participle/gerund 'fleeing' that modifies folgore; il proprio sito = 'its own place/site', so the image is of lightning fleeing its proper position.
segments.paradiso-1-g80: non corse is passato remoto 'did not run/move'; come introduces a comparison 'as'; tu ch' ad esso riedi is a relative clause 'you who return to it' (ch' = che 'who/that', ad esso riedi = 'you return to it'), so the whole line denies that the lightning moved in the way you imagine when you speak of its returning.
segments.paradiso-1-g81: S'io is an archaic contraction of se io ('if I'); fui ... disvestito is a passive/periphrastic past 'I was stripped (of)'; del primo dubbio = 'of the first doubt'; per le sorrise parolette brevi is an agent/means phrase 'by/through the smiling, brief little words'.
segments.paradiso-1-g82: dentro ad un nuovo = 'within/into a new (one)' (i.e. a new doubt); più fu' inretito = 'I was more ensnared' (fu = was, inretito = entangled/ensnared); e dissi = 'and I said' (passato remoto).
segments.paradiso-1-g83: Già contento requïevi is an observation in the first person: già 'already', contento 'satisfied', requïevi here 'I rested/was at rest' (archaic form); di grande ammirazion = 'from/of great admiration' expressing the cause/state of that rest.
segments.paradiso-1-g84: ma ora ammiro = 'but now I marvel'; com' io trascenda is an exclamatory/subjunctive clause 'how I transcend' (trascenda is present subjunctive used for wonder); questi corpi levi = 'these light bodies' (levi = light/easy).
segments.paradiso-1-g85: Ond' (from onde) is the same connective as before, 'so/thereupon'; ella is the subject pronoun 'she', resuming the action.
segments.paradiso-1-g86: appresso d'un pïo sospiro = 'after a pious sigh' (appresso here meaning 'after' in a temporal sense; d' = di), pïo = 'pious/compassionate', sospiro = 'sigh'.
segments.paradiso-1-g87: li occhi drizzò ver' me: drizzò is passato remoto 'she directed/turned' and ver' (verso) me = 'toward me'; con quel sembiante 'with that expression', che madre fa sovra figlio deliro = a relative clause 'which a mother makes over (her) child's rapture/delight' describing the look.
segments.paradiso-1-g88: e cominciò = 'and (she) began', linking the gesture to the start of her speech; cominciò is passato remoto third-person singular.
segments.paradiso-1-g89: Le cose tutte quante is a generalizing subject 'all the things whatsoever'; hanno ordine tra loro is a present-tense general statement 'have order among themselves', i.e. things are ordered in relation to one another.
segments.paradiso-1-g90: e questo è forma = 'and this is a form/shape'; che l'universo a Dio fa simigliante is a subordinate clause 'that makes the universe like unto God' (fa = 'makes', simigliante = 'similar/like'), i.e. this order is the form by which the universe resembles God.

sentences.paradiso-1-s17: To transcend human nature cannot be expressed in words; therefore let the example suffice for him to whom experience preserves grace.
sentences.paradiso-1-s18: If I were only what you newly created, Love who rules the heavens — you know it — who raised me with your light.
sentences.paradiso-1-s19: When the wheel that you eternalize, desired by you, made me attentive to itself by the harmony that you temper and discern, then heaven seemed to me so kindled by the sun's flame that neither rain nor river nor lake ever spread out so much.
sentences.paradiso-1-s20: The novelty of the sound and the great light from their cause kindled in me a desire never before felt of such keen intensity.
sentences.paradiso-1-s13: A great deal is permitted there that here is not permitted to our powers, thanks to the place made specially for the nature of the human species.
sentences.paradiso-1-s14: I did not endure it long, nor so briefly that I did not see it sparkle all around, like iron that, glowing, comes out of the fire; and suddenly it seemed that day was being added to day, as one who could have heaven adorned with another sun.
sentences.paradiso-1-s15: Beatrice stood wholly fixed among the eternal wheels, her eyes intent; and I, looking at her, fixed my lights/gaze on them, remote up there above.
sentences.paradiso-1-s16: In her expression I became such within as Glaucus became when he tasted the herb that made him a consort in the sea of the other gods.
sentences.paradiso-1-s1: The glory of Him who moves all penetrates through the universe, and shines in one place more and less elsewhere.
sentences.paradiso-1-s2: I was in the heaven that takes more of His light, and I saw things that neither he who descends from up there knows nor can recount; because, by drawing itself near to His desire, our intellect sinks so deep that memory cannot go after it.
sentences.paradiso-1-s3: Truly, as much as I was able to make a treasure in my mind of the holy kingdom will now be the subject of my song.
sentences.paradiso-1-s4: O good Apollo, for this final labor make me a vessel of your power, so made, as you require when giving the beloved laurel.
sentences.paradiso-1-s25: Here the lofty creatures behold the trace of eternal value, which is the end to which the touched rule is made.
sentences.paradiso-1-s26: In the order that I speak of all natures are inclined, by different destinies, nearer to their principle and less near; whence they move to different ports across the great sea of being, and each one with an instinct given to it that carries it.
sentences.paradiso-1-s27: This one bears the fire of it toward the moon; this one is the mover in mortal hearts; this one draws the earth together into itself and gathers it; nor even those creatures that are outside of intelligence can shoot this bow, but those that have intellect and love do.
sentences.paradiso-1-s28: Providence, which arranges so much, makes of its light the heaven always quiet in which that which is most hurried turns; and now there, as to an appointed place, the power of that cord brings us what it shoots straight in a joyful direction.
sentences.paradiso-1-s5: Up to this point one yoke of Parnassus was enough for me; but now, with both, I must enter the remaining assembly.
sentences.paradiso-1-s6: Enter into my breast, and breathe your (inspiration) as when you drew Marsyas from the sheath of his limbs.
sentences.paradiso-1-s7: O divine power, if you lend yourself to me so much that I may manifest the shadow of the blessed realm marked on my head, you will see me come to the foot of your beloved tree, and crown myself with the leaves which the subject and you will make me worthy of.
sentences.paradiso-1-s8: So seldom, Father, is it won — whether to triumph as Caesar or as poet — a fault and shame of human desires, that the Peneian frond ought to bring forth joy upon the glad Delphic deity when anyone seeks it for himself.
sentences.paradiso-1-s29: It is true that, just as form often does not conform to the intention of art — because matter is deaf to answering — so from this course the creature sometimes departs, which has the power to bend, so portrayed, to another place; and just as one can see a fire fall from a cloud, so the first impulse casts it down, twisted, by false pleasure.
sentences.paradiso-1-s30: You should no longer be astonished, if I judge rightly, at your ascent — except as at a stream that from a high mountain descends down to the very bottom.
sentences.paradiso-1-s31: It would be a marvel in you, if — free from impediment — you had been seated down below, as one at rest on the earth in living fire.
sentences.paradiso-1-s32: From here he turned his face toward the sky.
sentences.paradiso-1-s9: A little spark follows a great flame: perhaps, after me, with better voices people will pray that Cirra answer.
sentences.paradiso-1-s10: The world's lamp rises to mortals through various outlets; but from that one which reaches four circles with three crosses it issues joined with a better course and a better star, and worldly wax more in its own way tempers and seals.
sentences.paradiso-1-s11: It had made, on that side morning and on this side evening, such an outlet; and that hemisphere was there almost all white, and the other part black, when I saw Beatrice turned on my left side and looking at the sun: an eagle had never so fixed itself upon him.
sentences.paradiso-1-s12: And just as a second ray is wont to issue from the first and rise upward, even like a pilgrim who wishes to return, so from her act — poured through the eyes into my imagination — mine was made; and she fixed her eyes on the sun beyond our accustomed sight.
sentences.paradiso-1-s21: So she, who saw me just as I saw myself, to calm my agitated spirit, before I could ask, opened her mouth and began: "You yourself make yourself dull with false imagination, so that you do not see what you would see if you had shaken it off."
sentences.paradiso-1-s22: You are not on earth, as you believe; but lightning, fleeing its own place, did not move as you (think it) returns to it.
sentences.paradiso-1-s23: If I was stripped of my first doubt by those smiling short words, I was caught in a new one even more, and I said: 'Already content, I had been at rest in great admiration; but now I marvel how I transcend these light bodies.'
sentences.paradiso-1-s24: So she, after a pious sigh, directed her eyes toward me with that expression which a mother makes over her child's rapture, and began: "All things whatsoever have an order among themselves, and this is a form that makes the universe like unto God."
